figures.caption: Table 1. Ridge Open-Set TAR@FAR=10−4 (%) Across k TAR@FAR=10−4 for each model over varied k, dataset, and projection method. ISP-W refers to the ISP projection being calculated on the same dataset, and ISP-X refers to the ISP projection being calculated from the other dataset. Results are averaged over 5 different seeds. FR models are bolded; ISP is not applied to FR encoders (— indicates not applicable).

Figure 2. Template Inversion Verification Rate (%). Heatmap of verification rates across four attack families (DiffMI, Bob, Vec2Face, ALSUV) and five encoders. The FR encoder (ArcFace, top row) achieves consistently high rates (67–100%), while nonFR encoders (CLIP, DINOv2, DINOv3, SSCD) remain near zero across all methods. All rates averaged over ArcFace (τ=0.1051) and AdaFace (τ=0.1111) verifiers.

Figure 3. FCR Attribution: RAW vs ISP Overlay. (A) FII comparison: ISP reduces face-driven similarity. (B) Censoring comparison: context dominance maintained. (C) CPI delta: dramatic shift from context-only (1.0) to balanced (0.35-0.41). All images were cropped to a FCR of 0.33, with ±2% equal-area tolerance.

Figure 4. Template Inversion Gallery. Target vs. DiffMI reconstruction across five encoders. ArcFace reconstructions verify at high rates; non-FR encoders do not. Protocol: DiffMI white-box, 1,000-latent pool, APGD 100 iterations, N=200 CelebA-20 targets.

Table 11. Attribute-swap identity inference at k = 128. Joint = fraction of victims where identity is preserved (sim ≥ τFAR=10−3 ) and gender is flipped. FR models allow highly reliable joint manipulation; non-FR encoders do not. 500 victims per direction on CelebA-20.
